EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave of low pressure passed by to the south on the 20th. At the same time, high pressure was building to the north and this caused cold air to filter in from New England. Rain changed to a wintry mix on the 20th. There was enough cold air for a period of heavier snow around midday.  A light wintry mix continued on the night of the 20th. Coastal low pressure developed along the Mid-Atlantic Coast on the morning of the 21st. This caused a round of heavier precipitation to fall mainly in the form of snow. The snow tapered off later in the day as the low moved off to the north and east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 6.7 inches near Falling Waters and 6.0 inches near Martinsburg on the 20th. Another round of snow occurred on the 21st and a storm total of 14.0 inches was reported near Falling Waters and also near Martinsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable environment set the stage for convection during the afternoon hours. Conditions were ripe enough that very little forcing was required and therefore the main impulse to trigger the storms was orographic lift, with additional storm development initiated through intersecting outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds brought down a couple of trees onto Route 12 near I-64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy downpours over central Mercer County produced flash flooding in several small basins near Athens, WV. Dual-pol radar estimated more than 4 inches fell in a 3-hour period ending around 600 PM EST and an automated gauge measured 4.53 inches in Athens of which 3.5 inches fell in one hour from 420 to 520 PM EST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on several small creeks, including Laurel Creek and Tracy Fork north and east of Athens and at the town water treatment plant. Several private bridges were destroyed in the area near Red Sulphur Turnpike near Athens. A section of Red Sulphur Turnpike was badly damaged and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of daytime heating and an approaching upper level trough led to scattered thunderstorm development on the afternoon and evenings of the 26th, 27th and 28th. While these storms were slow moving, most moved enough to limit flooding. However, several storms stalled out or kept regenerating over one spot, leading to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in the Cross Lanes area. This included Doc Bailey Road near Kelly Road. Along Armour Creek, sections of Cross Lanes Drive, 40th Street and Timber Creek Lane flooded. Three homes also had minor water damage, two had a few inches of water on the main flow, and the third had a flooded basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As subtropical storm Alberto moved into the Mississippi Valley, strong thunderstorms formed in the outer bands of the storm over the middle Ohio River Valley on the 30th. As the remnants moved towards the Great Lakes, additional showers and storms moved through on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poplar Fork flooded, with water covering roads and yards in Beechwood Estates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As subtropical storm Alberto moved into the Mississippi Valley, strong thunderstorms formed in the outer bands of the storm over the middle Ohio River Valley on the 30th. As the remnants moved towards the Great Lakes, additional showers and storms moved through on the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of weak low pressure interacting with a stalled frontal boundary along with the soggy air mass produced very heavy rainfall across parts of Monroe County. Union IFLOWS (UNNW2) had 3.07 most of which fell in several hours. Residents described the flooding as 'the worst they had ever seen'.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Indian Creek was reported to be out of its banks and flowing across Route 219 at a depth of at least two feet in the Salt Sulphur Springs area. At least four homes in the same area received flood damage, including a bed and breakfast. The Union Rescue Squad office was also flooded. Several other creeks were reported to be flooded closing more roads.  Route 122 was reported with water over the road in the Raines Corner area. The Indian Creek gauge near Red Sulphur Springs (INCW2) located downstream of the heaviest rain area, crested at 11.62 feet, above the Minor flood stage of 8 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of sunshine earlier in the day, thunderstorms formed across southwest Virginia and southeast Kentucky during the late afternoon of the 5th as a low pressure system approached. These storms moved into southern West Virginia that evening, producing hail.

A cluster of slow-moving thunderstorms over the central portion of the county produced 3 to 4 inches of rain in about a 3 hour period as estimated by radar north of White Sulphur Springs and near Williamsburg. An IFLOWS gauge at the Williamsburg VFD (WMBW2) measured 2.96 inches storm total rainfall ending 12z (0700 EST) on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports said water was over Boones Mountain Road near Frankford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed on the afternoon of the 21st as a warm front lifted across the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians.  One of these produced wind damage, and other very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Right Fork of the Holly River caused high water on Guardian Drive. There was also a mudslide across another section of Guardian Drive. Along the Left Fork of the Holly River, flooding washed out a section of Polling Road. A nearby automated rain gauge reported 3.17 inches of rain in the hours prior to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a period of sunshine earlier in the day, thunderstorms formed across southwest Virginia and southeast Kentucky during the late afternoon of the 5th as a low pressure system approached. These storms moved into southern West Virginia that evening, producing hail.

A wave of low pressure moved along a nearly stalled front late on the 6th, with an upper level shortwave trough moving over head. The combination of these features produced a band of heavy, persistent rainfall. One to two inches of rain fell in 6-12 hours. With saturated ground in place from previous rain, this led to flooding of small creeks, streams and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Route 39 was flooded near Mill Creek due to high water on the Tygart Valley River.  ||The gage at Mill Creek rose above its 11 foot bankfull level just after midnight on the 6th. The river crested at 13 feet just before sunrise and flooding slowly subsided into the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed east of Cleveland from a breeze off of Lake Erie near 17Z. Areas south and east of the line gave way to more storms as outflow boundaries converged. With high PWAT values (near 1.90) flash flooding became an issue with training storms.  |Wet microbursts were also a concern with DCAPE values near 500kjg-1. The Weather Prediction Center had our region under a slight risk for excessive rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Highways reported trees down on property just north of HWY-72.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of daytime heating and an approaching upper level trough led to scattered thunderstorm development on the afternoon and evenings of the 26th, 27th and 28th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following about three inches of rain in under three hours, flash flooding along Scary Creek, Hurricane Creek, Crooked Creek and Poplar Fork lead to significant flooding of roads, homes and businesses in the Teays Valley and Scott Depot areas. Especially hard hit were the areas of Beechwood Estates, Maplewood Estates and Barrington Woods. All sit right along Poplar Fork. Five water rescues were performed in eastern Putnam County, this includes three separate vehicles that drove into high water. About 80 houses were damaged by the flooding, with five sustaining major damage. Two businesses sustained damage, the Scott Depot Post Office and a karate studio. Eight vehicles were destroyed, and another 10 had some water damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven Mile Creek flooded, causing the closure of Big Seven Mile Road. Rush Hollow Road also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in an unstable airmass on the afternoon of the 22nd, a couple of these storms produced wind damage. Later that evening a cold front pushed through with additional showers and storms. The multiple rounds of rain led isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at the Billy Dunn Baseball field and another along Kanawha Terrace in the Saint Albans area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front continued to linger in the vicinity of the Mason-Dixon Line.  A marked temperature contrast existed across the front, with values in the 50s over northwestern Pennsylvania and numbers well into the 80s over northern West Virginia. This boundary helped to produce more severe weather during the afternoon and evening hours.  Storms with hail larger than quarters and downed trees were reported, along with isolated flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacted with a moist and unstable air mass to produce thunderstorms across the region. Some of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMRB gusted to 54 knots (62 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly approaching frontal boundary late on the 7th triggered more widespread shower and thunderstorms in the late afternoon into the nighttime hours. Storms over Greenbrier County during the early morning hours of the 8th produced radar estimated rainfall of 1.5 to 3 inches in a few hours with some flash flooding reported near Lewisburg as water entered a home. For the 24 hours ending at 12z, Keeney Knob IFLOWS (KEEW2) measured 2.68��� (of which 1.85��� fell in 1 hour ending 05z) and Lewisburg COOP (LWBW2) 2.00���.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rainfall caused water to enter at least one home and wash away the all the gravel at a construction site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low and associated area of surface low pressure approached the region from southwest on the 15th. Strong forcing for ascent and ample moisture transport in advance of the upper level low led to a prolonged period of rain across the region. Eventually low pressure transferred to the coast before precipitation wound down on the 16th. Widespread rainfall totals of 2-4 inches were observed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Potomac River at Shepherdstown exceeded the 15 foot flood stage during the indicated period. River Road was completely flooded in low spots between Shepherdstown and Harpers Ferry. Water covered the pedestrian bridge to the Shepherdstown boat ramp and significant portions of the C&O canal were flooded. Water approached homes on the West Virginia side of the river near Shepherdstown and impacts to roads and structures were also observed on the Maryland side of the river. The peak level of 22.11 feet occurred at 3:00 AM on December 17th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the South Branch Potomac River at Springfield exceeded the 15 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Flooding was observed along Mileson Mill Road (County Route 28/5) and Clarence Taylor Road (Route 3/7). A significant portion of the meadowlands near the river, as well as the boat launch ramp were also flooded. The peak level of 16.25 feet occurred at 10:30 AM on December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon led to a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. This caused some flooding in western West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two roads were closed due to flooding near Milton. This included the 1600 block of John's Creek Road along Johns Branch, and 1700 block of Newman's Branch Road, along Mill Creek and Lower Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon led to a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. This caused some flooding in western West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middle Island Creek at Little rose above its 14 foot flood stage on the morning of the 10th. The creek crested in moderate flooding that afternoon at almost 17 feet before returning to its banks late that night. This flooded low areas on both sides of the creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Lewisburg 3 N (LWBW2) had 2.00 inches in the 24-hour period ending at 700 AM local time on September 8th which is the highest ever on this date. The old record was 1.90 inches in 1969. Nearly continuous climate records at this station date back to 1900.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 2700 Block of Williamsport Pike was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed with the passage of a cold front. There was sufficient moisture, modest instability, and weak wind shear. Forecasted soundings predicted 0-3km lapse rates at an excess of 8.5 C/km. Mid-level dry air helped create an environment for strong downdrafts/damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported street flooding.

Localized flooding continued to be the concern as the region has already had a number of rainy days prior. A Flash Flood Watch was issued by the office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved across the Upper Ohio river valley; there was broad areas of rainfall associated with this system. A flood watch was covered in Tucker, Garrett, and Eastern Preston County due to recent heavy rainfall has saturated the ground. Radar estimates ranged from 0.5 inches to 1.25 inches in Tucker/Garrett/Preston. The positioning of the upper level jet (right entrance region) allowed for for rain bands to be moderate to heavy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 Call Center reported flooding at an underpass; flooding at intersection of Gibson St and Bank St.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of I-64 east bound was closed just before the Oakwood Road exit due to high water. A portion of Barlow Drive was undermined by water flowing off the hill near Yeager Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following immediately on the heels of the system that produced flooding on the 20th in VA, another wave of low pressure tracked up along the spine of the Appalachians on the 21st. Showers and thunderstorms moved across the area during the afternoon hours of the 21st, locally producing an additional 1-2 inches of rain on top of previously saturated soils. This rainfall eventually led to some larger-stem river flooding on the 22nd and 23rd as the runoff drained downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the Shenandoah River at Millville exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Portions of Bloomery Road, John Rissler Road, Wilt Road as well as a few rural roads in Clarke County, VA experienced flooding. Moulton Park, the Millville boat launch, and portions of the Cool Spring Battlefield also flooded. The peak level of 12.56 feet occurred at 6:00 PM on the 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the Cacapon River at Great Cacapon exceeded the 9 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Rock Ford Road flooded in multiple locations, including flowing completely over the low water bridge. Flooding was also observed along Constant Run Road. The peak level of 9.28 feet occurred at 1:00 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon led to a prolonged period of moderate to heavy rain. This caused some flooding in western West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several of the viaducts in Huntington were closed, including the 8th and 10th Street viaducts. Third and Fifth Streets were also closed in the 2400 blocks due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 23rd a surface boundary was stalled across southern West Virginia. It slowly moved north as a warm front on the 24th and 25th as a surface low crossed the region. This low then pushed a cold front through on the 26th and 27th. Atmospheric moisture was anomalously high throughout the period, leading to multiple rounds of moderate to heavy rain which caused flooding issues across parts of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sycamore Lick Road was impassable due to flooding along Sycamore Lick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving frontal boundary triggered thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall in pockets across southwest Monroe and northeast Greenbrier counties. Rainfall estimates were 2 to 3 inches in several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding took place in the Peterstown area as  home along WV Highway 12 northwest of town was flooded and had to be evacuated as water rose in the home. A car in Peterstown was submerged and the passengers rescued. Extensive flooding also occurred in the Linside area with several cars flooded and abandoned along Route 219 and a water rescue of several passengers from one car. A home was badly damaged in Linside as a basement wall collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gordon came ashore along the central Gulf Coast on the night of September 4th.  The tropical cyclone dissipated over Missouri by the night of September 7th. Moisture from the decaying tropical system interacted with a surface low and frontal boundary, spreading widespread heavy rainfall across the region over a 3 day period. 72 hour totals across northern West Virginia ranged from 2 inches to about 5.5 inches.Small-stream flooding was not as widespread as in areas to the north and east in Pennsylvania, with only a few issues reported in Marshall County.  However, winds of 30-35 MPH combined with the saturated ground to cause a tree to fall on a house in Cheat Lake, killing a woman who was sleeping in an upstairs bedroom.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter rainfall total of 5.70 inches from Friday (Sept 7th) to 2:16pm Sunday (Sept 9th).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Gordon came ashore along the central Gulf Coast on the night of September 4th.  The tropical cyclone dissipated over Missouri by the night of September 7th. Moisture from the decaying tropical system interacted with a surface low and frontal boundary, spreading widespread heavy rainfall across the region over a 3 day period. 72 hour totals across northern West Virginia ranged from 2 inches to about 5.5 inches.Small-stream flooding was not as widespread as in areas to the north and east in Pennsylvania, with only a few issues reported in Marshall County.  However, winds of 30-35 MPH combined with the saturated ground to cause a tree to fall on a house in Cheat Lake, killing a woman who was sleeping in an upstairs bedroom.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 4.95 inches of rainfall in 72 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 23rd a surface boundary was stalled across southern West Virginia. It slowly moved north as a warm front on the 24th and 25th as a surface low crossed the region. This low then pushed a cold front through on the 26th and 27th. Atmospheric moisture was anomalously high throughout the period, leading to multiple rounds of moderate to heavy rain which caused flooding issues across parts of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Campbells Creek near Tad flooded creating high water on Campbells Creek Road.

Morning soundings showed very little upper level flow. A static atmosphere prompted a risk for flooding; storms that developed tended to move very little. Isolated wet microbursts were a potential due to CAPE values over 2000 J/kg. Storms developed from outflow boundaries and light convergence aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported that trees and powerlines were donw along Herring Road and Bull Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving frontal boundary triggered thunderstorms producing heavy rainfall in pockets across southwest Monroe and northeast Greenbrier counties. Rainfall estimates were 2 to 3 inches in several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of flash flooding across Greenbrier County. The tunnel on Route 63 at Howards Creek was flooded as the creek was out of its banks. A car was reported stuck in flood waters in the tunnel. Route 92 was flooded in various locations and there were several mud and rockslides along the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low and associated area of surface low pressure approached the region from southwest on the 15th. Strong forcing for ascent and ample moisture transport in advance of the upper level low led to a prolonged period of rain across the region. Eventually low pressure transferred to the coast before precipitation wound down on the 16th. Widespread rainfall totals of 2-4 inches were observed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the Cacapon River at Great Cacapon exceeded the 9 foot flood stage during the indicated period. Rock Ford Road flooded in multiple locations, including flowing completely over the low water bridge.  Flooding was also observed along Constant Run Road, Kilgore Road and Running Waters Way. The peak level of 13.05 feet occurred at 10:15 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed around a stalled boundary. A slow storm motion combined with copious amounts of moisture led to heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Tennessee Avenue was closed near est Race street due to flooding. Baltimore Street was closed due to flooding near Randolph Street. The 200 Block of Stephen Street was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow pumped in plenty of moisture from the Atlantic while a slow moving cold moved into the area. Copious amounts of moisture ahead of the boundary led to showers and thunderstorms. Torrential downpours combined with convection training over the same areas led to flooding along the Opequon Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on the Opequon Creek at Martinsburg crested at 11.77 feet, above the flood stage of 10 feet. Water covered backyards of homes along Bowers Road. Douglas Grove Road was flooded and impassible. Water also approached Golf Course Road and Grapevine Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Henshaw Road (500 block) closed due to flooding along Mill Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper level low helped to trigger one severe thunderstorm during the afternoon.  This storm produced hail up to the size of quarters in Monroe County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell at the UTC Aerospace Systems north of Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance moved through West Virginia on the 3rd. This resulted in showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain, leading to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Left Fork of Sandy Creek flooded north of Sandyville. A cooperative observer in the area measured 2.32 inches of rain in the hour prior to the flooding. Farther south in Jackson County, Mill Creek flooded causing high water along Route 33 between Ripley and Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance moved through West Virginia on the 3rd. This resulted in showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain, leading to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 5 was flooded near Ivan as Nettle Run Creek overflowed its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front in conjunction with an upper-level shortwave trough kicked off showers and thunderstorms on the 7th. One of these storms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding in Braxton County. Another storm caused minor wind damage in Putnam County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small lightweight metal building was blown over a fence and lawn furniture was knocked around. A large rotted tree and a limb of a Bradford pear tree were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon storms dropped 2 to 3 inches of rain in a few hours which led some flash flooding in Princeton and near Athens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Bluestone Dam recorded 1.90 inches for the 24 hours ending at 7 AM on the 31st of August, the highest ever for the date and 6th highest in the month of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon storms dropped 2 to 3 inches of rain in a few hours which led some flash flooding in Princeton and near Athens.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported flooded roads in Princeton including portions of Stafford Drive and Kirk Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance moved through West Virginia on the 3rd. This resulted in showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain, leading to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cow Creek flooded causing water to cover parts of Cow Creek Road. Indian Fork also flooded, forcing evacuations of the Westwood Mobile Home Community due to high water. Multiple homes were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed a line of showers and thunderstorms into parts of southeast West Virginia.  The thunderstorms produced wind damage by knocking down two trees in Mercer County before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down along Falls Mill Road, and one tree was blown down along Red Oak Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front propelled by a deep upper trough that took on a negative tilt, pushed across the region on April 15 producing severe weather and heavy rainfall. The speed of convective elements prevented flash flooding but longer duration flooding occurred on several rivers and streams, mainly in the minor category. Rainfall ranged from 2 to 3 inches in parts of southeast West Virginia in 24-hours ending at 7 AM on the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Indian Creek (INCW2) flooded cresting at 12.66 feet (Minor Flood stage - 8 ft.) early on the 16th, closing at least one road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with a negatively-tilted upper level trough approached southeast West Virginia.  Plenty of wind shear with modest instability made conditions ripe for damaging winds.  One thunderstorm would produce winds that caused damage in Summers County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew over several trees and billboards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front in conjunction with an upper-level shortwave trough kicked off showers and thunderstorms on the 7th. One of these storms produced heavy rainfall and flash flooding in Braxton County. Another storm caused minor wind damage in Putnam County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 4 was closed in multiple locations due to high water and mudslides. This included the Route 4 interchange with I-79 in Gassaway and also in several spots in Sutton. Other small roads in Gassaway were also closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and upper-level low pressure system brought showers and thunderstorms to central West Virginia on the morning of the 12th.  The strongest storms resulted in isolated wind damage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty homeowners lost power due to a tree falling on power lines near the town of Elana.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain created high water issues in the area, including 242 Edgle Fork Road near Clover and in Graux where vehicles were submerged with water to the top of their wheels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and upper-level low pressure system brought showers and thunderstorms to central West Virginia on the morning of the 12th.  The strongest storms resulted in isolated wind damage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of flooding and strong winds associated with a passing thunderstorm caused multiple trees and a power line to fall down on Otto Road near Graux.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and storms associated with a cold front passed through West Virginia the evening of the 9th and produced flooding issues in southeastern Harrison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive rainfall caused Brushy Fork to rise out of its banks and flood portions of Brushy Fork Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches occurred, with a local maximum of 6.2 inches measured near Middleway. Some ice accretion of up to two tenths of an inch also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the area on the 14th. The low tracked along a boundary that separated warm and humid air to the south and west from cooler marine air to the north and east. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a two day period caused flooding across eastern West Virginia on June 22nd into the 23rd. Then, the combination of upstream rainfall caused river flooding on the 23rd and 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads flooded and closed near Upper Tract.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving upper level low pressure drifted northeastward near the southern Great Lakes on June 22nd.  Disturbances rotating around this system combined with abundant moisture to create showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall. Wetzel County reported isolated flooding and road damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that State Route 2 in New Martinsville, near the river bridge, had about one foot of water on it, with some damage to the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening as a warm front surged northward, a band of heavy rain developed in Berkeley County, producing 1-2.5 inches of rainfall. This caused minor stream flooding near Martinsburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Tuscarora Creek in Martinsburg briefly exceeded the flood stage of 5.5 feet during the indicated times. Some minor flooding occurred in the city of Martinsburg near the stream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another band of heavy rain fell in Grant County on May 21st, with 1-3 inches of rain causing flooding in the southeastern part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple areas of water from South Mill Creek flowing over Durgon Road between North Mill Creek Road and the Hardy County border. Some were as much as two feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper level low pressure system brought showers and thunderstorms on the 2nd. An area of heavy rain across the northern mountainous counties of WV caused minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water closed multiple roads across the county. This included sections of Georgetown Road, Pritt Lane and Rack Road in Daily. Water also closed Becky Creek Road near Lee Bell and Route 29 near Whitmer. Adolph Road was washed out in several places near Adolph, and was also closed by mud slide debris near Mill Creek.||The river gage on the Tygart Valley River at Mill Creek showed the Tygart Valley River surpassed its bankfull level of 11 feet around noon on the 2nd. The river crested at 12.4 feet late that evening and returned to its banks during the predawn hours of the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1 to 4 inches fell on June 1st across the eastern West Virginia panhandle, causing flooding and a few instances of flash flooding in Hampshire County. Flooding continued into the overnight hours. Then, persistent rain on the 2nd renewed the flooding, along with isolated flash flooding in and near Pendleton County, where over three inches of rain was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide triggered by heavy rain, along with associated floodwaters, caused the closure of US 220 just south of Moorefield near the Valley View Golf Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-3 inches on saturated soil caused flooding in Morgan County, West Virginia on June 3rd. Separately, the combined effects of several days of rain created river flooding on streams which continued in some locations through June 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Warm Springs Run near Berkeley Springs exceeded the 5 foot flood stage during the indicated period. The peak level of 5.83 feet occurred at 10:30am EST on June 3rd. Video and photos from the area show significant inundation at Berkeley Springs State Park. Several roads and buildings were reported flooded along with fields at area schools.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on the Cacapon River near Great Cacapon exceeded the 9 foot flood stage during the indicated period. The peak level of 16.24 feet occurred at 10:15pm EST on June 3rd. Rock Ford Road was flooded and had to be closed for an extended period. Significant flooding with multiple road closures occurred throughout the floodplain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-3 inches on saturated soil caused flooding in Morgan County, West Virginia on June 3rd. Separately, the combined effects of several days of rain created river flooding on streams which continued in some locations through June 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight roads were closed, mainly in the northern part of the county, due to flooding. All of the roads were near streams which were out of their banks. Two rock and mud slides were reported, one across Cacapon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate rain over a two day period totaled 1-3 inches, and was enough to produce some flooding during the afternoon of May 16th. Additional heavy rain affected the far eastern West Virginia panhandle during the evening hours of the 16th, producing 3-5 inches of rain and causing additional flooding. Moderate rain continued through the 18th, causing stream flooding beginning the 17th and continuing through the early morning hours of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gaston Road flooded and closed either due to flooding of North River or backwater flooding of the Cacapon River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches occurred, with a local maximum of 10 inches measured near Lineburg. Some ice accretion of up to a tenth of an inch also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed along the Gulf Coast on the night of November 23rd, and tracked northeastward to the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 24th, strengthening to around 990mb on the night of the 24th as it continued moving northeastward through the Delmarva to off the coast of New Jersey by the morning of the 25th. The system then began weakening on the 25th as it moved out into the western Atlantic Ocean. Widespread rain was brought to the region, along with a period of freezing rain and ice accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread ice accumulations of between a tenth of an inch and a quarter of an inch of ice were observed, with the highest amounts reported near Short Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. The warm front associated with the low moved through the area while the cold front associated with the low approached from the west. There was enough lift associated with the warm front to combine with a warm and humid atmosphere, resulting in showers and thunderstorms. Rainfall was heavy at times which resulted in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 400 Block of Queen Street was closed under the underpass due to flooding from a creek. Parts of West John Street were closed due to flooding. Williamsport Pike was closed between Berkley Station Road and Mid-Atlantic Parkway due to flooding. Parts of Baltimore Street were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary cluster of thunderstorms centered between Princeton and Spanishburg dropped rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 inches. Most of the rain fell within about a three-hour period from 830 to 1130 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Princeton COOP measured 5.78 inches for the 24 hours ending at 7 AM on July 30th which was an all-time daily rainfall record at this station. The old record was 5.00 inches on January 22, 1906.  Precipitation records exist from 1900-1914 and 1940 to the present at the site. Per NOAA Atlas 14 this was approximately a 500-year (.002 annual exceedance probability) recurrence interval for a 24-hour period, although most of the rain fell over a much shorter duration, roughly 3 to 5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the cloud morning, an approaching short wave trough, crossing during the afternoon and evening hours, helped spark showers and thunderstorms on the 22nd. A slow moving surface front became the focus for convection as the trough approached, with downdraft CAPE values near 800j/kg.  Storms initially formed across northern Ohio and then moved southeast through eastern Ohio and western PA. The hardest hit areas where Tuscarawas county in Ohio and Hancock county in northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported power lines and at least 30 trees down along Allison Road.

The peak level observed was 10.63 feet on May 19th at 4:30pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low and associated area of surface low pressure approached the region from southwest on the 15th. Strong forcing for ascent and ample moisture transport in advance of the upper level low led to a prolonged period of rain across the region. Eventually low pressure transferred to the coast before precipitation wound down on the 16th. Widespread rainfall totals of 2-4 inches were observed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Opequon Creek at Martinsburg exceeded the 12.5 foot flood stage during the indicated period. This pushed the creek well out of its banks, leading to flooding in the nearby lowlands. Fields and backyards near the creek flooded, along with many local roads. Golf Course Road (County Road 36) was flooded near the Van Metre Ford Pedestrian Bridge. Several other roads experienced flooding, including Grapevine Road, Douglas Grove Road, and Paynes Ford Road. Backwater flooding also occurred on small tributaries, including Tuscarora Creek. The peak level of 13.52 feet occurred at 3:30 PM on December 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate rain over a two day period totaled 1-3 inches, and was enough to produce some flooding during the afternoon of May 16th. Additional heavy rain affected the far eastern West Virginia panhandle during the evening hours of the 16th, producing 3-5 inches of rain and causing additional flooding. Moderate rain continued through the 18th, causing stream flooding beginning the 17th and continuing through the early morning hours of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Opequon Creek near Martinsburg exceeded the 10 foot flood stage between the times indicated. The peak river level was the highest recorded since 2014, reaching 14.36 feet at 6pm EST on May 17th. Numerous roads flooded within the designated area, along with backyards and outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed by to the west toward the Great Lakes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued on Warm Springs Run near Berkeley Springs. Floodwaters were no longer rising rapidly, but earlier flooding persisted for several hours in Berkeley Springs State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked eastward along the Gulf Coast and spread moisture northward toward the Mid Atlantic.  Meanwhile, high pressure to the north allowed temperatures to fall into the 20s, which caused the moisture to mostly fall as heavy snow.  Some freezing drizzle also fell during this storm.  The snow caused several minor traffic accidents.  Average snowfall accumulations ranged from ten to twenty inches over southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 9.0 inches at Neola to 18.0 inches at Alderson.  Several minor traffic accidents occurred due to the snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 13.0 inches at Bramwell to 18.0 inches at Oakvale.  Bluefield received 15.0 inches of snow, which is the second highest storm total in the month of December since records began in 1909.  It is also the earliest in the winter season that an amount this high has ever been recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell to the north of a stalled front on September 9th and 10th. One to three inches of rain fell across the eastern West Virginia Panhandle late on the 8th into the 9th, followed by another one to three inches on the 9th into the 10th. This combination produced fairly widespread flooding, including of the mainstem rivers, which continued through the 11th and into the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Henshaw Road was closed due to flooding at the I-81 underpass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches occurred, with a local maximum of 4.5 inches measured near Cherry Grove. Some ice accretion of up to a tenth of an inch also occurred.

Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough approached the area on the 24th as surface low pressure tracked up toward the Great Lakes and secondary low pressure formed along the Carolina Coast. Lift ahead of the negatively tilted trough/within the left exit region of an upper-level jet streak led to a period of moderate to heavy rain through much of the day on the 24th. Rainfall totals were generally between 1-2 inches across the area, resulting in many instances of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage on the Opequon Creek at Martinsburg exceeded the 10 foot flood stage during the indicated period, leading to flooding along portions of Douglas Grove Road (County Road 9/16) and  Bowers Road.  Additionally, water covered the stream access point at the Van Metre Ford Pedestrian Bridge. The peak level of 10.32 feet occurred at 9:30 AM on the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed along the Gulf Coast on the night of November 23rd, and tracked northeastward to the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 24th, strengthening to around 990mb on the night of the 24th as it continued moving northeastward through the Delmarva to off the coast of New Jersey by the morning of the 25th. The system then began weakening on the 25th as it moved out into the western Atlantic Ocean. Widespread rain was brought to the region, along with a period of freezing rain and ice accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations of up to a tenth of an inch were observed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians on the 20th. This brought a round of gusty winds which led to scattered tree damage and power outages. One of the hardest hit counties was Kanawha, where over 14,000 customers lost power. The ASOS at Yeager Airport measured a wind gust of 45 mph. A home along Toledo Avenue in Kanawha City has some siding blown off due to the winds, and a home in South Charleston had a punctured roof after a tree fell on it. It took nearly two days for all the power outages to be fixed.||Several thousand power outages also occurred in Cabell, Logan, Lincoln, Mason, Putnam, Raleigh and Wayne Counties. The ASOS at Raleigh County Airport measured a gust of 49 knots.||AEP reported a total of 17 transmission lines were taken out in West Virginia, knocking multiple substation offline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold surface temperatures were already in place across the region at least 24 hours leading up to this event.  Surface high pressure allowed high temperatures to only reach near freezing while low temperatures bottomed out in the mid-20s. A closed upper level low formed just north of Texas on November 14th and tracked northeast. An abundant amount of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico traveled with the system into the northeast United States. Freezing rain eventually transitioned to snow when northwest flow began on the backside of the surface low. Four to seven inches of snow was measured north of I-80 and in the higher elevations, meanwhile 1 to 3 inches of snow happened elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow accumulated in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the middle Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians early on the the 6th. There was little to no lightning, but a line of showers just ahead of the front contained some strong wind gusts. The strongest winds occurred in a bowing shower segment that crossed from Wayne and Cabell Counties into Lincoln, Putnam and Kanawha Counties before fading out. This blew down trees and caused nearly 10,000 power outages across the southwestern part of the state. The 6th was Election Day, and generators had to be placed at some polling stations to run the voting equipment before power was restored. Yeager Airport measured the highest wind gust, 48 mph, with many other measurements topping out in the 35-45 mph range.

Southwest winds on the east side of an advancing area of low pressure brought warm and moist air across the region the night of November 14th into the morning of November 15th. This warm and moist air resulted in rain falling across the area, but falling through a shallow layer of below freezing air just above and at ground level, and onto surfaces also below freezing. The result was a freezing rain event that deposited up to one-quarter of an inch of ice on trees, power lines, and roads. At least 12,000 customers were out of power because of the ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretion totals across the county included 0.25 inches at a location two miles west of Hollywood.

A strong low pressure moved up the East Coast on the 15th. Cold air was pushed up against the eastern slopes of the Appalachians, with warmer air flowing in aloft. This lead to a period of freezing rain across the eastern slopes. Around a quarter inch of ice accumulated on trees and power lines across parts of Fayette, Pocahontas, Raleigh, and Randolph counties.  For example, 0.33 was measured by a trained spotter in Cool Ridge in the far eastern part of Raleigh County, and the ASOS at the Raleigh County airport accumulated 0.34 on its sensor. A quarter of an inch accumulated in Bowden in Randolph County, and 0.30 in Marlinton in Pocahontas County. This lead to widespread power outages, Raleigh County was hit especially hard with over 8000 customers without power. Some had to wait several days to service to be restored.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved up the eastern seaboard of the United States from November 26th through the 27th, with cold temperatures and strong northwest winds funneling behind the system from the night of the 27th into the morning of the 28th. This was able to produce wind chill values as low as -10 degrees over the higher terrain near the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold temperatures and strong winds produced wind chills as low as -10 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the southeastern United States on the night of November 14th, and tracked northeastward along the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 15th, strengthening to around 995mb on the night of the 15th as it continued moving northward through the Delmarva to New Jersey by the morning of the 16th. Further strengthening to around 985 mb occurred on the 16th as the system sped up and moved towards Nova Scotia. Widespread precipitation was brought to the region, including heavy snow and mixed precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 7 to 10 inches occurred, with a local maximum of 10.1 inches measured near Keyser. Some ice accretion of up to a tenth of an inch also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed along the Gulf Coast on the night of November 23rd, and tracked northeastward to the North and South Carolina coastlines during the daytime hours on the 24th, strengthening to around 990mb on the night of the 24th as it continued moving northeastward through the Delmarva to off the coast of New Jersey by the morning of the 25th. The system then began weakening on the 25th as it moved out into the western Atlantic Ocean. Widespread rain was brought to the region, along with a period of freezing rain and ice accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trace amounts of ice were observed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty northwest flow behind a departing low pressure system combined with lingering low level moisture and elevated terrain to generate up-slope snow showers along and near the Allegheny Front from the afternoon of November 27th into the morning of the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of up to 4 inches were observed across portions of Western Grant County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several low pressure systems lifted through the Appalachians between the 20th and 24th. On the 21st, an area of heavy rainfall moved across western West Virginia with totals of 1 to 1.25 inches over 24 hours. The higher amounts were along the Ohio River. On the 22nd, another widespread 0.75 to 1.25 inches fell, with up to 1.5 inches across the southern Coal Fields. All of this rain fell on already saturated ground, and quickly became runoff into local creeks and streams, pushing some into flooding. Eventually, this water worked its way into the Ohio River, with minor flooding in typical low spots along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding of small creeks and streams. This included Cedar Crest Drive along Cedar Creek, Green Valley Drive along Grapevine Branch, and the intersection of Fudges Creek Road and Little Fudges Creek Road along Fudges Creek. The 10th Street Viaduct in downtown Huntington was also closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region late on the 1st, kicking off showers and thunderstorms. One of these storms became severe with localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down at the intersection of Route 74 and Haddox Run Road northeast of Pennsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southern stream low pressure system crossed the Gulf States on the 8th and 9th, moving out to sea late on the 9th. This storm brought significant snowfall to the central and southern Appalachians. The snow started early on the 9th and continued through the afternoon. The hardest hit areas were the eastern portions of Fayette, Raleigh, Wyoming and McDowell Counties where a blanket of 10 to 18 inches of snow fell.  Across the western parts of those counties, and into eastern Mingo and Logan Counties generally 6 to 10 inches fell. The highest report came from McGraws in Wyoming County where 18.5 inches of snow was measured. The cooperative observer at Oceana in Wyoming county measured 11 inches, and the observer at R.D. Bailey Lake had 8 inches. The 911 Center in Raleigh County, near Stanaford, measured 12 inches, with 17 inches measured near Glade Springs. The cooperative observer in Beckley got 9 inches. In Fayette County, the highest amount was 13 inches in Layland, but on the western side of the county, the cooperative observer at Hawks Nest only received 2 inches of snow. In McDowell County, 16.5 inches was measured in Anawalt, 12.5 inches in Pageton and 10 inches in Welch. Near Delbarton in Mingo County, 7 inches of snow fell on the top of Buffalo Mountain, with 6 inches near Williamson on Airport Mountain. The Department of Highways reported widespread amounts of 6 to 8 inches across southern and eastern Logan County, with only an inch across the north. The cooperative observer in Man got 4 inches.

This storm brought significant snowfall to the central and southern Appalachians. The snow started early on the 9th and continued through the afternoon. The hardest hit areas were the eastern portions of Fayette, Raleigh, Wyoming and McDowell Counties where a blanket of 10 to 18 inches of snow fell.  Across the western parts of those counties, and into eastern Mingo and Logan Counties generally 6 to 10 inches fell. The highest report came from McGraws in Wyoming County where 18.5 inches of snow was measured. The cooperative observer at Oceana in Wyoming county measured 11 inches, and the observer at R.D. Bailey Lake had 8 inches. The 911 Center in Raleigh County, near Stanaford, measured 12 inches, with 17 inches measured near Glade Springs. The cooperative observer in Beckley got 9 inches. In Fayette County, the highest amount was 13 inches in Layland, but on the western side of the county, the cooperative observer at Hawks Nest only received 2 inches of snow. In McDowell County, 16.5 inches was measured in Anawalt, 12.5 inches in Pageton and 10 inches in Welch. Near Delbarton in Mingo County, 7 inches of snow fell on the top of Buffalo Mountain, with 6 inches near Williamson on Airport Mountain. The Department of Highways reported widespread amounts of 6 to 8 inches across southern and eastern Logan County, with only an inch across the north. The cooperative observer in Man got 4 inches.

Several low pressure systems lifted through the Appalachians between the 20th and 24th. On the 21st, an area of heavy rainfall moved across western West Virginia with totals of 1 to 1.25 inches over 24 hours. The higher amounts were along the Ohio River. On the 22nd, another widespread 0.75 to 1.25 inches fell, with up to 1.5 inches across the southern Coal Fields. All of this rain fell on already saturated ground, and quickly became runoff into local creeks and streams, pushing some into flooding. Eventually, this water worked its way into the Ohio River, with minor flooding in typical low spots along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rainfall lead to high water in the low spot on Mud Fork Road near US 119.
